title: Anesthesia for Orthopedics MCQ – Chapter 36
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav

questions.question: What special problems does orthopedic anesthesia pose?

options.C: Lengthy procedures, increased blood loss, positioning, tourniquet-related problems, fat embolism and DVT

questions.explanation: Orthopedic anesthesia poses special problems due to lengthy procedures, increase blood loss, positioning, tourniquet-related problems, complications like fat embolism and deep vein thrombosis.

questions.question: What complications are associated with tourniquet during inflation?

options.B: Hypertension, increased cardiac output and increased pulmonary artery pressure

questions.explanation: During inflation: Hypertension, increased cardiac output and increased pulmonary artery pressure. This is due to increased blood volume associated with exsanguination.

questions.question: What metabolic change occurs within 6 minutes of tourniquet inflation?

options.A: Local tissue ischemia leads to acidosis (mitochondrial pO2 becomes zero)

questions.explanation: Metabolic changes: Local tissue ischemia leads to acidosis (mitochondrial pO2 becomes zero within 6 minutes of tourniquet inflation).

questions.question: How is pain during tourniquet inflation relieved?

options.A: Only by deflation (not relieved by IV analgesics)

questions.explanation: Pain is due to cellular acidosis, therefore may not be relieved by intravenous analgesics. It is relieved only by deflation.

questions.question: What causes hypotension during tourniquet deflation?

options.B: Sudden release of accumulated metabolites like thromboxane and substance P

questions.explanation: Hypotension because of sudden release of accumulated metabolites like thromboxane and substance P.

questions.question: Why does hypothermia occur during tourniquet deflation?

options.A: Due to heat loss as acid metabolites are vasodilators

questions.explanation: Hypothermia due to heat loss as acid metabolites are vasodilators.

questions.question: What happens to end tidal CO2 during tourniquet deflation?

options.D: Transient increase (CO2 accumulated in limb is released)

questions.explanation: Increase in end tidal CO2 (which is accumulated in limb).

questions.question: Which is the most crucial period during tourniquet use?
questions.explanation: Deflation is the most crucial period. Cardiac arrest may occur at the time of deflation due to hypotension and release of acid metabolites.

questions.question: What is the maximum duration for tourniquet pressure for upper and lower limbs?

options.B: Not more than 2 hours for both upper and lower limbs

questions.explanation: Pressure in tourniquet should not exceed more than 2 hours for both upper and lower limbs.

questions.question: If re-inflation of tourniquet is needed, how long should it be deflated first?
questions.explanation: If re-inflation is needed, then it should be deflated for at least 30 minutes before re-inflating.

questions.question: What is the maximum tourniquet pressure above systolic?

options.B: Not more than 100 mm Hg above systolic (maximum 250 mm Hg)

questions.explanation: Pressure in tourniquet should not exceed more than 100 mm Hg above systolic (maximum-250 mm Hg).

questions.question: What complication may occur if tourniquet pressure is high or time is prolonged?
questions.explanation: Post-tourniquet: Neuropraxia may occur if tourniquet pressure is high or time is prolonged.

questions.question: When is fat embolism syndrome (FES) usually seen?

options.C: After fracture of long bones and pelvis

questions.explanation: Usually seen after fracture of long bones and pelvis. Incidence of significant embolism after long bone and pelvic fractures may be as high as 15%.

questions.question: When does fat embolism classically present after fracture?
questions.explanation: Fat embolism classically presents within 24-72 hours of fracture.

questions.question: Is the incidence of FES higher in nailing or plating?

options.B: Higher in nailing compared to plating

questions.explanation: Due to rimming the incidence of fat embolism syndrome (FES), is higher in nailing as compared to plating.

questions.question: What is the classic triad of fat embolism syndrome?
questions.explanation: The triad of fat embolism syndrome is dyspnea, confusion and petechial hemorrhages.

questions.question: What is pathognomonic of fat embolism syndrome?
questions.explanation: Petechial rash is pathognomonic of FES.

questions.question: What are the signs and symptoms of fat embolism?

options.B: Dyspnea, chest discomfort, tachycardia, hypotension, mental clouding, restlessness, seizures, delirium, petechial hemorrhage on upper chest, axilla and conjunctiva, hypoxia and finally coma and death

questions.explanation: Signs include dyspnea, chest discomfort, tachycardia, hypotension, mental clouding, restlessness, seizures, delirium, petechial hemorrhage on upper chest, axilla and conjunctiva, hypoxia and finally coma and death.

questions.question: What do fat globules in urine indicate regarding fat embolism?

options.C: Helpful in diagnosis but may not necessarily indicate progression to FES

questions.explanation: The fat globules in urine are helpful in diagnosis, but may not necessarily indicate the progression to fat embolism syndrome.

questions.question: What does X-ray chest show in fat embolism?
questions.explanation: X-ray chest shows diffuse interstitial infiltrates.

questions.question: What does capnography show in fat embolism?

options.D: Fall in end tidal CO2 (ETCO2 may become zero if embolus is large enough to block main pulmonary artery)

questions.explanation: Capnography shows fall in end tidal CO2 (ETCO2 may become zero if embolus is large enough to block the main pulmonary artery).

questions.question: What ECG finding is seen in fat embolism?
questions.explanation: ECG shows the right ventricular strain.

questions.question: What happens to serum lipase in fat embolism?

options.A: Increased (but has no relation to severity of disease)

questions.explanation: Serum lipase is increased (but has no relation to the severity of disease).

questions.question: What is the first step in treatment of fat embolism?

options.C: Early stabilization of fracture (may prevent fat embolism)

questions.explanation: Stabilization of fracture: Early stabilization of fracture may prevent fat embolism.

questions.question: What ventilatory support is used if hypoxia persists on oxygen therapy in fat embolism?

options.D: Intermittent positive pressure ventilation (IPPV) with positive end expiratory pressure (PEEP)

questions.explanation: If hypoxia persists on oxygen therapy consider intermittent positive pressure ventilation (IPPV) with positive end expiratory pressure (PEEP).

questions.question: What is the pathophysiology of fat embolism in the lungs?

options.B: Fat emboli cause endothelial injury which can precipitate ARDS and cerebral edema

questions.explanation: Fat emboli released from long bone causes endothelial injury in the lungs and brain, which can precipitate acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) and cerebral edema respectively.

questions.question: Are corticosteroids, heparin and ethanol considered effective treatments for fat embolism currently?

options.B: No, they are now considered as outdated treatment modalities

questions.explanation: Corticosteroids, heparin (which stimulates circulating lipase that breaks neutral fat), ethanol and hypertonic saline are now considered as outdated treatment modalities.

questions.question: What is the incidence of fatal pulmonary embolism (PE) after hip surgery?
questions.explanation: The incidence of fatal PE after hip surgery can be as high as 1-3%.

questions.question: In which orthopedic patients is the incidence of DVT and PE highest?

options.D: Hip surgery patients particularly after prolonged immobilization

questions.explanation: The incidence of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism (PE) is highest in orthopedic patients particularly after hip surgery due to prolonged immobilization.

questions.question: When should low molecular weight heparin be started for DVT prophylaxis in hip surgery?

options.D: 12 hours or more in preoperative period and restart 12 hours after surgery

questions.explanation: Many centers start low molecular weight heparin before 12 hours or more in preoperative period and restart 12 hours after surgery.

questions.question: What serious complications have been reported in shoulder surgeries done in beach chair position?

options.A: Stroke or even brain death due to ischemia

questions.explanation: There have been case reports of stroke or even brain death in shoulder surgeries done in beach chair position due to ischemia.

questions.question: Where should the arterial pressure transducer be zeroed for shoulder surgeries in beach chair position?

options.A: At the level of the hip
options.B: At the level of external auditory meatus to denote cerebral perfusion pressure
options.C: At the level of the arm
options.D: At the level of the heart

questions.explanation: Invasive blood pressure is highly recommended for shoulder surgeries done in the beach chair position with transducer zeroed at the level of external auditory meatus to denote cerebral perfusion pressure.

questions.question: What is cement implantation syndrome (CIS)?

options.A: Hypoxia, profound hypotension or even cardiac arrest occurring during cemented prosthesis in total hip replacement

questions.explanation: Cement implantation syndrome (CIS) occurs more commonly in total hip replacement where cemented prosthesis is used. There occurs hypoxia, profound hypotension or even cardiac arrest.

questions.question: What causes cement implantation syndrome?

options.D: Embolization of bone marrow debris, microemboli or fat due to increased intramedullary pressure or reaction to cement (methyl methacrylate)

questions.explanation: It may occur because of embolization of bone marrow debris, microemboli or fat due to increased intramedullary pressure created by cement or due to reaction to cement (methyl methacrylate).

questions.question: What is the treatment for cement implantation syndrome?

options.A: Oxygenation, adequate hydration and adrenaline for severe reactions

questions.explanation: The treatment includes Oxygenation, adequate hydration and adrenaline for severe reactions.

questions.question: Why is regional anesthesia always preferred over general anesthesia for orthopedic surgeries?

options.A: Due to decreased incidence of thromboembolism, decreased blood loss, fewer respiratory complications and better control of pain

questions.explanation: Regional anesthesia is always preferred over general anesthesia due to decrease incidence of thromboembolism, decrease blood loss, respiratory complications and better control of pain.

questions.question: What type of regional block is used for upper limb surgeries?
questions.explanation: Upper limb surgeries can be performed under brachial plexus block.

questions.question: What block can be used for small procedures lasting less than 30-45 minutes?
questions.explanation: Small procedures lasting less than 30-45 minutes can be performed under Bier's block.

questions.question: What is the most preferred technique for lower limb surgeries?

options.C: Spinal/epidural or combined spinal epidural (most preferred technique)

questions.explanation: Lower limb surgeries are performed under spinal/epidural or combined spinal epidural (most preferred technique).

questions.question: When is general anesthesia required for orthopedic surgery?

options.D: For very prolonged surgeries of upper limb, shoulder or spine surgeries or when regional is not possible

questions.explanation: General anesthesia is required either for very prolonged surgeries of upper limb, shoulder or spine surgeries or when regional is not possible.

questions.question: What nerve blocks are frequently utilized for postoperative analgesia in orthopedics?

options.C: Continuous adductor canal block for knee, fascia iliaca/femoral nerve block for hip surgeries, brachial plexus block for upper limb

questions.explanation: Continuous adductor canal block for knee replacement, fascia iliaca/femoral nerve block for hip surgeries, brachial plexus block for upper limb surgeries are frequently utilized for postoperative analgesia.

questions.question: In what position is spine surgery performed?
questions.explanation: Spine surgery is performed under general anesthesia in prone position.

questions.question: What are the complications related to prone position in spine surgery?

options.A: Endotracheal tube dislodgement, airway edema, injury to brachial plexus and peroneal nerve, injury to genitalia and breast, ocular injury (postoperative vision loss), hypotension

questions.explanation: Complications related to prone position include endotracheal tube dislodgement, airway edema, injury to brachial plexus and peroneal nerve, injury to genitalia and breast, ocular injury (post-operative vision loss), hypotension.

questions.question: What monitoring is required for major spine surgeries like scoliosis?

options.A: Monitoring of spinal cord function by evoked responses like SSEP, MEP and EMG

questions.explanation: Monitoring of spinal cord function is required for major spine surgeries like scoliosis. The function of the spinal cord is monitored by evoked responses like somatosensory evoked potential (SSEP), motor evoked potential (MEP) and electromyogram (EMG).

questions.question: Is wake up test done in current day practice for spinal cord monitoring?

options.A: No, it is not performed in current day practice

questions.explanation: Wake up test done in the past is not performed in current day practice.

questions.question: What should be the concentration of inhalational agents during evoked response monitoring?

options.A: Half minimum alveolar concentration (MAC) as inhalational agents can depress evoked responses

questions.explanation: As inhalational agents can depress these evoked responses their concentration should be kept at half minimum alveolar concentration (MAC).

questions.question: Do IV agents preserve evoked responses?

options.D: Yes, IV agents preserve evoked responses except a little inhibition seen with Propofol

questions.explanation: IV agents preserve evoked responses except a little inhibition seen with Propofol.

questions.question: Can muscle relaxants be used during MEP monitoring?

options.C: Yes, all muscle relaxants can be used
options.D: No, muscle relaxants cannot be used during MEP monitoring

questions.explanation: Muscle relaxants cannot be used during MEP monitoring.

questions.question: What can happen at the time of tourniquet deflation that is most dangerous?
questions.explanation: Cardiac arrest can occur at the time of tourniquet deflation.

questions.question: What coagulation changes are seen in fat embolism?

options.C: Increased clotting time and decreased platelets

questions.explanation: Coagulation abnormalities like increased clotting time and decreased platelets are seen in fat embolism.
